data.title: Scienture secures $1.2 million in bridge funding
data.description:
• Scienture (NASDAQ:SCNX) has approved a bridge funding raise of up to an aggregate amount of $3M, of which it has secured about $1.2M, through the issuance of shares of the company’s common stock to institutional or accredited investors.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Open Lending appoints Massimo Monaco as CFO
data.description:
• Open Lending (NASDAQ:LPRO) announced on Thursday the appointment of Massimo Monaco as Chief Financial Officer, effective August 18, 2025.
  • Most recently, Monaco served as CFO of Arc Home, a residential mortgage lender, from 2018 to 2025, following his role as CFO at American Financial Resources from 2016 to 2018.

data.title: Heritage Commerce and Heritage Bank of Commerce appoint Seth Fonti as CFO
data.description:
• Heritage Commerce (NASDAQ:HTBK), parent company of Heritage Bank of Commerce, announced on Thursday the appointment of Seth Fonti as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer of the company and the bank, effective July 24, 2025.
  • Fonti brings more than two decades of financial and strategic leadership experience. Most recently, he served as Managing Director and Head of Strategy, Corporate Development, and Strategic Finance for MUFG Americas.

data.title: Carnival Corporation snaps six straight sessions of gains
data.description:
Carnival Corporation (NYSE:CCL) shares snapped six straight sessions of gains on Thursday, as it lost 3.12% to close at $29.78 during afternoon trading.

The cruise operator gained nearly 6% in the preceding six sessions. The stock closed 2.84% higher at $30.74 on Wednesday.

In the year so far, the company has added nearly 22%, compared to an 8.12% rise in the broader S&P500 index. In the past year, Carnival Corporation’s shares have gone up by over 61%.

The stock’s fall on Thursday is likely due to profit booking by investors.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s quant rating, the company has a Strong Buy rating with a score of 4.94 out of 5, lifted by an A grade in profitability and momentum.

Seeking Alpha analysts had a Buy recommendation for the firm’s shares, and the Wall Street community echoed the sentiment with a similar rating. Overall, 22 analysts gave the company a Buy and above rating, while 8 recommended to Hold the stock and none rated it as Sell.

Earlier this month, Seeking Alpha analyst Manika Premsingh reiterated her Buy rating for the stock on the back of the company’s Q2 earnings beat and upgraded guidance, which she argued, supports continued optimism.

However, the analyst has cautioned against broader market sentiment which could hamper the stock’s growth.

“Despite further potential for CCL, it's still possible that it won't see the uptick indicated. That's because the mood of the markets can change. Even with its largely steady rise since April, the S&P 500 (SP500) has still risen by less than 7% this year,” Premsingh added.

data.title: Newmont in charts: Gold sales, production fall for second quarter in row
Weyerhaeuser Q2 earnings bolstered by real estate sales
data.description:
Weyerhaeuser (NYSE:WY) Q2 earnings rose from Q1 as adjusted EBITDA from its real estate, energy & natural resources division jumped due to the timing and mix of real estate sales.

Adjusted EBITDA at its wood products and timberland units declined from the prior quarter.

Q2 EPS of $0.12, topping the average analyst estimate of $0.10, rose from $0.11 in Q1 and dropped from $21 in last year’s Q2.

Net sales of $1.88B, beating the $1.84B consensus, increased from $1.76B in the previous quarter and declined from $1.94B in the year-ago quarter.

Lagging the $345M Visible Alpha consensus, Q2 adjusted EBITDA rose to $336M, exceeding from $328M in Q1 and fell $410M in last year’s Q2.

Weyerhaeuser (NYSE:WY) stock rose 0.7% in after-hours trading.

Its Timberlands unit’s adjusted EBITDA slipped to $152M from $167M in Q1. The division's Q3 earnings before special items and adjusted EBITDA will be ~$10M lower than Q2

Wood Products adjusted EBITDA of $101M vs. $161M in the prior quarter. Its Q3 outlook is for earnings before special items and adjusted EBITDA to be comparable to Q2, excluding the effect of changes in average sales realizations for lumber andoriented strand board.

Real Estate, Energy & Natural Resources adjusted EBITDA jumped to $143M from $82M. The company expects the unit's Q3 earnings before special items will be ~$60M lower than Q2's and adjusted EBITDA will be about $80M lower than Q2's, due to the timing and mix of real estate sales. It continues to expect that full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $350M.

Conference call on July 25, 15 10:00 AM ET.

data.title: Gold Royalty reports Q2 results
data.description: • Gold Royalty press release (NYSE:GROY): Q2 total Revenue, Land Agreement Proceeds and Interest increased by approximately 100% to $4.4 million (revenue of $3.8 million) from the second quarter of 2024, equating to 1,346 gold equivalent ounces (GEOs). For the first half of 2025, Total Revenue, Land Agreement and Proceeds and Interest also increased by 25% from the same period in 2024 to a record $8.0 million (revenue of $6.9 million), equating to 2,595 GEOs.

data.title: Healthpeak Properties stock dips after Q2 FFO stalls, as expected, 2025 guide maintained
data.description:
Healthpeak Properties (NYSE:DOC) shares drifted down 2.5% in Thursday after-hours trading after delivering second-quarter funds from operations that matched the consensus

The healthcare REIT maintained its full-year 2025 guidance for FFO per share, as adjusted, of $1.81-$1.87, in line with the average analyst estimate of $1.85.

Q2 2025 FFO per share, as adjusted, of $0.46, meeting the consensus, stalled from the prior quarter and inched up from $0.45 a year ago.

Q2 revenue of $694.4M, in Q2 vs. $693.9M expected, advanced from $687.1M in Q1 and ticked down from $695.5M in 2024;s Q2.

Costs and expenses totaled $651.6M in Q2, up from $642.5M in Q1 and down from $666.2M in the year-earlier quarter.

New and renewal lease executions totaled 1.5M square feet. Outpatient medical new and renewal lease executions represented 1M square feet, and lab new and renewal lease executions totaled 503K square feet.

During the quarter ended June 30, 2025, Healthpeak (NYSE:DOC) entered into two new development deals with a combined projected cost of $148M to support Northside Hospital’s continued outpatient expansion in the Atlanta market.

Meanwhile, it sold one outpatient medical land parcel in June 2025 and two outpatient medical buildings in July 2025 for combined proceeds of ~$35M.

Conference call on July 25 at 8:00 a.m. ET.

data.title: Valero anticipates wider sour crude differentials and $2B in 2025 capital investments as refining throughput hits record levels
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Valero Energy Corporation (VLO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO R. Lane Riggs emphasized, "We are pleased to report solid financial results for the second quarter, driven by our strong operational and commercial execution. In fact, we set a record for refining throughput rate in our U.S. Gulf Coast region in the second quarter, demonstrating the benefits of our investments in growth and optimization projects." Riggs noted that refining margins were supported by strong product demand and low global product inventories, highlighting, "early July U.S. diesel inventories and days of supply are at the lowest level for the month in almost 30 years."
  • Riggs added that quarterly diesel sales volumes were up approximately 10% over the same period last year, while gasoline sales were about the same as last year. He said, "we continue to progress the FCC unit optimization project at St. Charles that will enable the refinery to increase the yield of high-value products, including high-octane alkylate. The project is expected to cost $230 million in startup in 2026."
  • Riggs shared optimism for refining fundamentals given planned refinery closures and limited capacity additions beyond 2025, and expects sour crude oil differentials to widen as OPEC+ and Canada increase production in the coming quarters.
  • CFO Jason Fraser stated, "For the second quarter of 2025, net income attributable to Valero stockholders was $714 million or $2.28 per share compared to $880 million or $2.71 per share for the second quarter of 2024." He highlighted that the Refining segment reported $1.3 billion of operating income for the quarter, with throughput volumes averaging 2.9 million barrels per day, or 92% throughput capacity utilization. Fraser also noted, "Refining cash operating expenses were $4.91 per barrel in the second quarter of 2025."

Outlook

  • Fraser reaffirmed, "We still expect capital investments attributable to Valero for 2025 to be approximately $2 billion, which includes expenditures for turnarounds, catalysts, regulatory compliance and joint venture investments. About $1.6 billion of that is allocated to sustaining the business and the balance to growth."
  • For Q3, refining throughput volumes are expected within these ranges: Gulf Coast at 1.76 million to 1.81 million barrels per day; Mid-Continent at 430,000 to 450,000 barrels per day; West Coast at 240,000 to 260,000 barrels per day; and North Atlantic at 465,000 to 485,000 barrels per day.
  • Fraser guided, "We expect refining cash operating expenses in the third quarter to be approximately $4.80 per barrel."
  • The Renewable Diesel segment guidance remains at approximately 1.1 billion gallons of sales in 2025, with operating expenses of $0.53 per gallon. Ethanol production is expected to be 4.6 million gallons per day in Q3, with operating expenses averaging $0.40 per gallon.

Financial Results

  • Valero reported $714 million in net income attributable to stockholders or $2.28 per share. The Refining segment’s operating income was $1.3 billion with throughput volumes at 2.9 million barrels per day and 92% throughput capacity utilization. Refining cash operating expenses were $4.91 per barrel.
  • The Renewable Diesel segment posted an operating loss of $79 million, while sales volumes averaged 2.7 million gallons per day. The Ethanol segment generated $54 million in operating income, with 4.6 million gallons per day in production.
  • G&A expenses were $220 million, net interest expense $141 million, and depreciation and amortization $814 million, which included $100 million of incremental depreciation tied to the planned Benicia Refinery closure.
  • Net cash provided by operating activities was $936 million, with $1.3 billion in adjusted net cash provided when excluding certain joint venture and working capital items.
  • Capital investments attributable to Valero were $399 million for the quarter. The company returned $695 million to shareholders, with $354 million in dividends and $341 million in share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Theresa Chen, Barclays: Asked about refined product demand trends and export market signals. Gary K. Simmons responded, "fundamentals around refining continue to look very supportive... demand for transportation fuels remains robust, not only here in the U.S., but also in our typical export markets." He noted gasoline demand is flat year-over-year, while diesel sales are trending about 3% above last year.
  • Manav Gupta, UBS: Inquired about capacity additions and Gulf Coast capture performance. Simmons stated, "there's not a lot of new capacity coming online... most consultants were forecasting around 800,000 barrels a day of total light product demand growth, which would have indicated significant tightening starting next year." Greg Bram explained, "a lot of the improvement was driven by really strong operating performance coming out of the heavy maintenance we had in the first quarter."
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: Focused on capital returns and DGD (renewable diesel). Homer Bhullar said, "we've got our stated minimum commitment to an annual payout of 40% to 50% of adjusted cash flow... we'll continue to use all excess free cash flow to buy back shares." Eric Fisher commented, "long-term, there's still enough tailwind out there that says this segment will continue to be in demand."
  • Further questions covered distillate yield, renewable diesel economics, SAF operations, the Benicia Refinery closure, crude differentials, and regional market dynamics.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was inquisitive, with a focus on demand trends, capital returns, renewable diesel challenges, and regulatory developments. No overt skepticism or negativity was apparent, though questions pressed for clarity on market outlooks and capital allocation.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, with Riggs emphasizing, "we remain committed to maintain our track record of commercial and operational excellence," and Simmons describing refining fundamentals as "very supportive." In the Q&A, management addressed challenges but reiterated optimism and operational discipline.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious (due to heavy maintenance and margin pressures) to more confident, reflecting improved operational results and throughput metrics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for capital investments remains unchanged at $2 billion for 2025, with $1.6 billion for sustaining the business.
  • Q2 showed a return to net income ($714 million) after a Q1 net loss attributable to stockholders (impacted by asset impairment). Refining segment performance improved, with operating income rebounding from a Q1 loss to $1.3 billion in Q2.
  • Throughput volumes increased from 2.8 million to 2.9 million barrels per day, and capacity utilization rose from 89% to 92%.
  • Cash operating expenses per barrel improved from $5.07 in Q1 to $4.91 in Q2.
  • Renewable Diesel’s operating loss narrowed from $141 million in Q1 to $79 million in Q2, and sales volumes increased from 2.4 to 2.7 million gallons per day.
  • Management’s sentiment moved from defensive and cautious in Q1 to confident in Q2, driven by operational execution and market demand.
  • Analysts shifted from focusing on asset impairments and West Coast closures in Q1 to throughput records, demand, capital returns, and regulatory outlooks in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited uncertainty in the Renewable Diesel segment, with Fisher noting, "it's in a lot of policy clarity, vagueness right now... the linchpin in all of this is going to be what the EPA says post their comment period."
  • Incremental depreciation expenses tied to the planned Benicia Refinery closure were highlighted, expected to impact earnings in the coming quarters.
  • Analysts and management discussed potential impacts from OPEC+ production increases, refinery rationalization, and regulatory shifts affecting renewable markets.
  • Simmons indicated that gasoline cracks may follow typical seasonal patterns, with limited expected support absent supply disruptions.

Final Takeaway

Valero’s second quarter results reflected strong operational performance, record U.S. Gulf Coast refining throughput, and robust demand for diesel products. Management signaled continued discipline in capital returns and capital investments, while remaining optimistic on refining fundamentals amid expected wider sour crude differentials and limited new refining capacity. Challenges in the renewable diesel segment and regulatory uncertainties remain key watchpoints, but the company’s focus on operational excellence and financial flexibility underscores its forward strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
